title: RV8GR full T2 control opcode sweep
purpose: Prove the RV8GR T2 horizontal-control equations for every opcode and both initial Z states as a reusable circuit-level contract.

chips.role: IBUS/DBUS ownership and store/load bus safety
AC, Z, ALU result, and store buffer effect
PG and DP register load side effects
Opcode-sweep observation, phase, clock, and bus-conflict monitors

behavior.scope: This circuit tests pure T2 execution logic and intentionally bypasses T0/T1 fetch.
behavior.ibus: SRC=1 selects RAM[{DP,IRL}], otherwise IRL immediate.
behavior.alu: XOR_MODE selects AC feedback or {8{ALU_SUB}}; MUX_SEL selects XOR output or adder sum.
behavior.ac: AC_WR=1 captures the selected ALU result; AC_WR=0 holds AC.
behavior.z: Z updates only when AC_WR=1; otherwise it holds the initial Z.
behavior.pg: PG loads IBUS when MUX_SEL=1 and AC_WR=0. Reserved mixes are visible and are not hidden by extra decode.
behavior.dp: DP loads IRL when XOR_MODE=1, SRC=0, STR=0, and AC_WR=0.
behavior.ie: IE sets when SRC=1, XOR_MODE=0, and AC_WR=0.
behavior.pc: PC loads {PG,IRL} when JMP=1 or BR=1 and Z matches ALU_SUB.
behavior.ram: STR=1 writes AC to RAM when the data address is in RAM page.

sample_points: control bits stable before T2 execution edge
bus ownership checked before write/read side effects
destination registers checked after model propagation
unsafe_states: reserved opcode mix changes PG, DP, IE, PC, RAM, or AC in a way not described by physical decode
SRC and STR overlap creates unreported bus contention
PC loads outside the documented branch/jump equation

guarantees: All 512 opcode/Z cases match the RV8GR Verilog opcode-sweep equations
Named reserved mixes are explicit and visible
Bus ownership remains conflict-free for the physical T2 equations
No test may reduce the full sweep to a PASS banner without assertions

audience: students ages 10-15, still useful for older learners
